critic_raw:
The experiment conflates two independent factors: model architecture and feature set. The proposed model uses gradient-boosted trees with both clinical and SDOH features; the baseline uses logistic regression with clinical features only. Any observed improvement could be due to (1) gradient boosting being more powerful than logistic regression, (2) SDOH features adding genuine predictive signal, or (3) both. Without a third arm — gradient-boosted trees trained on clinical features alone — the SDOH contribution cannot be isolated from the architectural difference. This is the core design flaw: the hypothesis is about SDOH features, but the experiment cannot attribute improvement to them.

SDOH features like income, housing status, and education are subject to missingness not at random (MNAR). Patients with unstable housing are less likely to have housing status recorded in EHRs than patients with stable housing. This means the missingness pattern is correlated with the outcome (readmission risk). Fitting an imputer on training data and applying it to test does not correct for MNAR bias — it assumes missingness is at random, which it is not for SDOH variables. The model will systematically underestimate risk for patients whose SDOH data is missing.

Stratified random split on multi-year hospital data does not account for hospital policy changes over time. If a hospital implemented new readmission-reduction programs partway through the five-year window, the model will train on pre-program data and test on a random mixture, failing to capture the genuine temporal distributional shift. A temporal split would surface this.
all_issues_raised: The proposed model and baseline differ in both architecture (gradient boosting vs. logistic regression) and feature set (clinical + SDOH vs. clinical only), making it impossible to attribute any observed improvement to SDOH features rather than model complexity — a third arm with gradient boosting on clinical features alone is required.

SDOH features (income, housing, education) are missing not at random in EHRs — missingness is correlated with the outcome since patients with unstable housing are less likely to have housing data recorded — and fitting an imputer on training data does not correct for this MNAR bias.

Stratified random split on five years of hospital data does not account for readmission-reduction policy changes over time, mixing pre-policy and post-policy observations across splits and obscuring whether the model generalizes to the current clinical environment.
all_issues_adjudicated: The proposed model and baseline differ in both architecture (gradient boosting vs. logistic regression) and feature set (clinical + SDOH vs. clinical only), making it impossible to attribute any observed improvement to SDOH features rather than model complexity — a third arm with gradient boosting on clinical features alone is required.

SDOH features (income, housing, education) are missing not at random in EHRs — missingness is correlated with the outcome since patients with unstable housing are less likely to have housing data recorded — and fitting an imputer on training data does not correct for this MNAR bias.

Stratified random split on five years of hospital data does not account for readmission-reduction policy changes over time, mixing pre-policy and post-policy observations across splits and obscuring whether the model generalizes to the current clinical environment.
